one_line_takeaway: By identifying and selectively poisoning only a small set of 'backdoor-critical' layers, then smoothing/blending those updates toward the benign-client average, LSA achieves backdoor success rates of ~88-97% against six FL defenses including FLAME and Multi-Krum, substantially outperforming BadNets, DBA, and the prior layer-targeted attack LPA (Zhuang et al.) while largely preserving main-task accuracy.
key_finding: Under FLAME (described as the strongest/most robust defense tested), LSA on ResNet-18 achieves BSR=96.98% with 72.19% main-task accuracy, versus BadNets (BSR=7.22%) and DBA (BSR=3.88%) under the same defense. On VGG-19 under Multi-Krum, LSA reaches BSR=88.08% with 82.23% accuracy versus the LPA baseline's BSR=56.81% with 69.37% accuracy. Across the full defense x architecture grid (Table IV), LSA's BSR exceeds 88% in most settings and is the highest of the four attacks in nearly every cell (exceptions: FLTrust on VGG-19, where LSA's BSR of 71.45% trails; and CNN/FedAVG and CNN/FLTrust, where BadNets/DBA already saturate near 99-100% BSR with no defense pressure). On main-task accuracy under LSA vs. attack-free FL (Table III, ResNet-18), LSA does not consistently degrade accuracy relative to the no-attack baseline -- in several defenses (Median, Trimmed-Mean, Multi-Krum) accuracy under LSA is actually higher than the attack-free baseline (e.g., Multi-Krum: 73.49% attack-free vs. 78.52% under LSA), which the authors attribute to LSA's stealth mechanism. Stealthiness against FLAME's detector (Table II) shows LSA achieves the highest Malicious Acceptance Rate among all attacks on every architecture (e.g., ResNet-18: LSA MAR=89.50% vs. LPA=57.00%, BadNets=5.58%, DBA=3.50%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces the Layer Smoothing Attack (LSA), a backdoor-injection attack for federated learning (FL) built on two novel pieces: (1) an enhanced 'Layer Substitution Analysis' methodology that systematically identifies a compact set of 'backdoor-critical' (BC) layers -- found by measuring the BSR drop when a layer of a malicious model is substituted into a benign model (delta-BSR_b2m), sorting layers by this contribution, and greedily building a minimal layer set L* via the inverse (malicious-into-benign) substitution until BSR reaches a threshold tau of the fully-malicious BSR; and (2) LSA itself, which poisons only the BC layers, fine-tunes just those layers (freezing the rest) to resolve distributional inconsistencies, and blends the fine-tuned malicious layers with an estimate of the benign-user average update via a selection vector v and a stealth hyperparameter lambda (with a ReLU-based constraint on the blend) to make the malicious update statistically resemble benign updates. LSA also updates its target BC-layer set only periodically (e.g. every 5 rounds) rather than every round, to cut computational cost.
threat_model: Federated learning with 100 users, 10% (M) of them malicious/colluding, of whom 10% of total users are sampled for local training each round. Malicious clients receive the current global model each round, have local datasets they can split into clean/poisoned subsets, can communicate/coordinate strategy, and can locally estimate benign-client model updates (as in prior work such as Fang et al. and 3DFed) to make their own uploaded updates statistically resemble benign ones. The paper explicitly restricts to a minority-malicious setting (fewer than half of users), noting FL defenses are largely ineffective once a majority is compromised. The attacker's goal: cause the aggregated global model to misclassify trigger-containing inputs to an attacker-chosen target class (high Backdoor Success Rate) while (a) preserving clean-task accuracy and (b) evading server-side FL defenses (robust aggregation and anomaly-detection mechanisms).
limitations: The paper does not report results for LSA against FLTrust on VGG-19 with lambda/tau retuned per-defense (all hyperparameters were tuned only on ResNet-18/FLAME and then reused everywhere, which likely explains the one dataset/defense combination -- VGG-19/FLTrust -- where LSA underperforms LPA). Evaluation is confined to image classification (CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST) and three CNN-family architectures; no NLP, tabular, or transformer-based FL settings are tested. The attack assumes malicious clients can estimate the benign-user average update locally (citing prior poisoning-attack literature for this capability) -- the paper does not independently validate how accurate this estimate needs to be for the stealth mechanism to work, nor does it test against defenses designed specifically to counter such update-mimicry. No formal detectability/statistical-distance analysis is given for why the smoothed updates evade FLAME's anomaly scoring beyond the empirical MAR/BAR numbers. The paper only briefly evaluates the layer-detection-period tradeoff (Fig. 4) without quantifying the wall-clock cost savings numerically in the text. As an ICC 2026 conference paper the evaluation is simulation-only; no real IoT/edge deployment or communication-overhead measurements are reported.

quantitative_results: LSA on ResNet-18 under FLAME: BSR=96.98%, ACC=72.19%
BadNets on ResNet-18 under FLAME: BSR=7.22%
DBA on ResNet-18 under FLAME: BSR=3.88%
LSA on VGG-19 under FedAVG: BSR=96%, ACC=76.56%
LSA on VGG-19 under Multi-Krum: BSR=88.08%, ACC=82.23%
LPA on VGG-19 under Multi-Krum: BSR=56.81%, ACC=69.37%
LSA MAR under FLAME on ResNet18/VGG19/CNN: 89.50% / 97.00% / 97.00%
LPA MAR under FLAME on ResNet18/VGG19/CNN: 57.00% / 65.50% / 46.50%
BadNets MAR under FLAME on ResNet18/VGG19/CNN: 5.58% / 16.58% / 0.25%
DBA MAR under FLAME on ResNet18/VGG19/CNN: 3.50% / 12.25% / 0.50%

ResNet-18 main-task accuracy attack-free vs. under LSA (Table III): FedAVG 77.26% vs 75.18%; Median 77.37% vs 79.61%; Trimmed-Mean 77.02% vs 78.09%; Multi-Krum 73.49% vs 78.52%; FLTrust 74.61% vs 75.24%; Flame 71.10% vs 72.76%
Best hyperparameters found: lambda=1, tau=0.8 (tuned on ResNet-18 under Flame)
baselines_compared: DBA - Distributed Backdoor Attack (Xie et al.)
LPA - Layer-wise Poisoning Attack (Zhuang et al., cited as prior work, not this paper's contribution)
